ideas.The dichotomy of control:
The "**dichotomy of control**" is the foundational principle of Epictetus's **Stoic** philosophy and the very first teaching of his handbook, the *Enchiridion* — arguably the single most practically influential idea in the entire Stoic tradition. It is the recognition that some things are "up to us" (*eph' hēmin*) and other things are not, and that the whole of wisdom, freedom, and tranquility depends on learning, sharply and consistently, to tell the two apart.

"**Some things are within our power, while others are not.**" Within our power, Epictetus teaches, are our own **judgments, opinions, desires, aversions, and choices** — in a word, the workings of our own mind and will, our *prohairesis* (our faculty of choice). *Not* within our power is everything else: our body, health, reputation, wealth, status, other people's actions and opinions, and all external circumstances and events. These externals we can influence but never fully control; they are ultimately not "ours."

The transformative insight is that nearly all human suffering comes from the fundamental error of investing our hopes, fears, and sense of well-being in the things we *cannot* control — and then being disappointed, anxious, and enslaved by fortune's turns. The remedy is to relocate our concern entirely to what *is* in our power: to work relentlessly on our own judgments and choices, which alone are truly free, and to accept externals with equanimity as beyond our command. "Men are disturbed not by things, but by their opinions about things." By caring only about what is genuinely up to us, we become invulnerable to fortune and attain a serene, unshakable freedom. This simple but demanding discipline is the heart of Epictetus's teaching, and the root of modern Stoicism's practical appeal.
ideas.Inner freedom of a slave, inner slavery:
The paradox that a **slave can be inwardly free while a king is inwardly enslaved** is one of the most powerful themes of Epictetus's philosophy — and one he embodied in his own life, for Epictetus was himself born a **slave** in the Roman Empire before gaining his freedom and becoming a revered teacher. From this hard experience he drew a radical redefinition of **freedom** that severs it entirely from external condition.

For Epictetus, true freedom has nothing to do with one's legal or social status, and everything to do with the state of one's **mind and will**. Following the dichotomy of control, he argues that the only thing genuinely "ours," genuinely within our power, is our own faculty of choice (*prohairesis*) — our judgments, desires, and assents. A person who has mastered this inner domain, who desires only what is up to them and accepts everything external with equanimity, is **free** in the deepest sense, no matter how constrained their outward circumstances — even in chains. Nothing external can compel or disturb them, for they have withdrawn their well-being from fortune's reach.

Conversely, a **king** or a wealthy, powerful person — who *appears* to have everything and to command others — may in truth be utterly **enslaved**: a slave to his own fears, cravings, ambitions, and to the countless externals (his reputation, his possessions, the emperor's favor) on which he has staked his happiness and which he cannot control. Trembling before what he might lose, ruled by his passions, he is more truly in bondage than the free-minded slave. Real slavery is thus internal — the surrender of one's judgments to externals — and real freedom is self-mastery. This teaching gives Stoicism its profound consolation for the powerless and its warning to the powerful.
ideas.Live according to your assent, not appea:
A central practical discipline in Epictetus's Stoicism is the training of **assent** — the teaching that we must not be ruled by mere **appearances** (*phantasiai*, impressions), but must examine and judge them before giving or withholding our agreement. This is the mechanism by which the dichotomy of control is put into daily practice, and it locates human freedom precisely in the moment of judgment.

Epictetus observes that we are constantly bombarded by **impressions** — a person insults us, a loss occurs, a temptation presents itself — and that each impression comes bearing an implicit *value judgment* ("this is terrible," "this is an injury," "I must have this"). The untrained person automatically and unreflectively **assents** to these appearances, accepting their verdict as reality, and is thereby thrown into distress, anger, or craving. But the appearance and its judgment are two different things, and the judgment is *ours*. "It is not things that disturb us, but our judgments about things."

The Stoic discipline, therefore, is to insert a pause between impression and assent. When a troubling impression arises, we should say to it: "You are just an impression, and not at all what you claim to be" — and then test it against reason and the dichotomy of control. Does this concern something up to me or not? Is the value judgment it carries actually correct? By scrutinizing our impressions and withholding assent from false or disturbing ones, we take command of the one thing truly in our power. We stop being passively jerked about by how things *appear* and instead live by our own considered, rational assent. This vigilant guarding of the mind's assent is the core practical exercise of Epictetan Stoicism.

works.Discourses (recorded by Arrian):
The *Discourses* (*Diatribai*) are the principal record of Epictetus's teaching — a collection of his informal lectures and conversations, transcribed by his devoted student **Arrian**, who insisted he was faithfully setting down his master's actual words and manner of speaking rather than composing a polished treatise. Since Epictetus, like Socrates, wrote nothing himself, we owe our knowledge of his philosophy almost entirely to Arrian's records. Four of the original eight books survive.

The *Discourses* capture Epictetus in action as a teacher in his school at Nicopolis, addressing his students in a vivid, direct, often blunt and humorous style. Rather than presenting abstract Stoic theory systematically, they show philosophy as a way of life being hammered out in practice — with Epictetus exhorting, questioning, chiding, and encouraging his listeners to actually *apply* Stoic principles to their daily struggles, fears, and relationships. He returns again and again to his central themes: the **dichotomy of control** (distinguishing what is "up to us" from what is not); the disciplining of **desire**, **action**, and **assent**; the true nature of **freedom** as inner self-mastery; the acceptance of the divine order of the cosmos; and the proper fulfillment of one's social roles and duties.

Throughout, the tone is practical and morally urgent: philosophy is medicine for the soul, a lecture-room is a "hospital," and the point is not to admire fine arguments but to be transformed by them. Rich in memorable examples, analogies, and imagined dialogues, the *Discourses* are among the most engaging and inspiring texts of ancient philosophy, and they preserve the living voice of one of Stoicism's greatest teachers.
works.Enchiridion:
The *Enchiridion* (Greek for "**handbook**" or, literally, "something held in the hand") is a short, concentrated manual of Epictetus's ethical teaching, compiled by his student **Arrian** as a distillation of the essential practical doctrines from the longer *Discourses*. Compact enough to be carried and consulted constantly, it was designed as a ready guide to living the Stoic life — and it has become, over the centuries, one of the most widely read and enduringly influential works of practical philosophy ever written.

The *Enchiridion* opens with the foundational principle that governs everything that follows — the **dichotomy of control**: "Some things are within our power, while others are not. Within our power are opinion, motivation, desire, aversion... not within our power are our body, our property, reputation, office." From this single distinction flows the whole of Stoic wisdom: focus entirely on what is up to you (your own judgments and choices), and accept with equanimity everything that is not.

In a series of pithy, memorable chapters, the handbook then applies this principle to the practical business of living: how to handle desire and aversion, loss and grief, insults and other people's opinions; how to regard death, illness, and misfortune ("Men are disturbed not by things, but by their judgments about things"); how to play one's assigned role in life well, as an actor plays a part chosen by the playwright; and how to maintain tranquility and virtue in all circumstances. Free of technical jargon and full of vivid images, the *Enchiridion* has consoled and guided readers for nearly two millennia — from Roman times through Christian monasteries to modern soldiers and the founders of cognitive therapy — as the quintessential pocket-guide to Stoic living.
